module: Module 3: Boolean Expressions and if Statements

sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Three — boolean expressions and if statements. This is the moment your programs stop running in a straight line and start making decisions. By the end of this lesson, your code can branch.

Think of a subway turnstile. If your balance is greater than or equal to the price, the gate opens. Otherwise, it blocks you. That single sentence — if condition, then action, else other action — is the entire structure of computer decision-making. Every game, every login, every checkout is built on it.

Three things to cover. One — boolean expressions, the comparisons and logic that produce true or false. Two — if, else, and else-if, how Java actually branches. Three — the double-equals versus dot-equals trap for Strings, the biggest gotcha in this unit.

Six relational operators. Greater than, less than, greater-or-equal, less-or-equal, double-equals for equality, and exclamation-equals for not-equal. Each one returns a boolean — true or false. Example. int age equals seventeen. boolean canVote equals age greater-or-equal eighteen. That evaluates to false. That's it. Comparison gives you a boolean.

Here's the biggest gotcha in this whole module, and I'm previewing it now so it sticks. Double-equals compares references for objects. For Strings, you must use dot-equals. Two strings that look identical can fail a double-equals check if one was created with the new keyword. We'll come back to this on the compare slide. For now, just remember — Strings use dot-equals.

Three logical operators. Double-ampersand is AND — both sides must be true. Double-pipe is OR — at least one side must be true. Exclamation point is NOT — it flips a boolean. Example. age greater-or-equal thirteen AND age less-or-equal nineteen. That's true if the person is a teenager.

Java short-circuits. If the left side of an AND is false, the right side is never even evaluated. This saves you. Look at if b not-equal zero AND a divided by b greater than one. Safe — no divide-by-zero. Reverse the order and your program crashes. Order matters.

De Morgan's laws. Pure logic gold. NOT of A AND B equals NOT A OR NOT B. NOT of A OR B equals NOT A AND NOT B. Whenever you need to negate a compound condition, these two rules tell you how to flip it cleanly. You will use them on the exam.

Your turn. Write a boolean expression that is true exactly when age is between thirteen and nineteen inclusive. Hint — both bounds must hold, so this is an AND. Pause now. Write it. Press play when you're ready.

Time. The answer is age greater-or-equal thirteen AND age less-or-equal nineteen. Watch out — in Java you cannot write thirteen less-or-equal age less-or-equal nineteen. That looks like math, but it's a compile error. Java needs two separate comparisons joined with AND.

Now the if statement itself. Syntax — if open-paren condition close-paren curly-brace, do something, close-brace. Optionally followed by else, with its own curly-braces. Example. if score greater-or-equal sixty, print Pass. Else, print Fail. That's the basic two-way branch.

Multiple branches need an else-if ladder. Java checks them in order, top to bottom, and stops at the first true condition. If score is ninety or more, grade A. Else if eighty or more, grade B. Else if seventy or more, grade C. Else, F. Order is everything — put the strictest condition first.

You can also nest. An if inside another if. Useful, but readability drops fast. If logged-in is true, then check if is-admin. Real talk — most of the time, a single AND condition is cleaner than two layers of nesting. Reach for AND first, nest only when you really need separate else branches.

And here we are — the comparison trap I warned you about. On the left, wrong. if name double-equals quote Alex quote. This may return false even when name looks identical to Alex. Double-equals compares references, not contents. On the right, correct. if name dot equals quote Alex quote. Always use dot-equals for Strings. Always.

Recap. Six relational operators all return a boolean. Double-ampersand, double-pipe, and exclamation point build compound expressions. Short-circuit evaluation saves you from crashes. De Morgan's laws flip compounds. If, else-if, else for branching. And — Strings use dot-equals, never double-equals. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master booleans and if statements, here's the rest. Read the Boolean Expressions chapter on Runestone — interactive code blocks. Practice Logic-One on Codingbat — ten problems. Submit the assignment in your dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor if any of this feels fuzzy. See you in Module Four — Iteration.
